preamble.summary: God prescribes the full sacrificial calendar: the daily tamid offering (morning and evening), the Sabbath addition, the new moon offerings, and the spring festival cycle — Passover, the Festival of Unleavened Bread, and the Festival of Weeks (Shavuot). Each occasion specifies exact numbers of bulls, rams, lambs, grain offerings, and drink offerings.
preamble.remarkable: The opening command calls the offerings qorbani lachmi ('My offering, My food,' v. 2) — anthropomorphic language that does not claim God eats but frames the sacrificial system as sustaining the covenantal relationship. The qorban (from qarav, 'to draw near') is fundamentally about approach to God's presence. The tamid ('perpetual, continual') offering structures every day around divine encounter: morning and evening, without exception, the relationship is renewed.
preamble.friction: The KJV renders minchah as 'meat offering,' which is misleading — minchah is specifically the grain offering, never meat. We rendered it consistently as 'grain offering.' The measurement terms (issaron for one-tenth ephah, hin for liquid measure) we converted to ratios rather than modern equivalents, since the exact volumes are debated. The phrase reiach nichoach ('pleasing aroma') we retained as a theological metaphor for divine acceptance.
preamble.connections: The tamid offering was established in Exodus 29:38-42. The Passover regulations (vv. 16-25) supplement Exodus 12 and Leviticus 23:5-8. The Festival of Weeks (vv. 26-31) corresponds to Leviticus 23:15-21 and later becomes Pentecost (Acts 2:1). The sacrificial quantities here establish the norms that will govern temple worship through the Second Temple period.

verses.text_hebrew: וַיְדַבֵּ֥ר יְהֹוָ֖ה אֶל־מֹשֶׁ֥ה לֵּאמֹֽר׃
verses.text_kjv: And the LORD spake unto Moses, saying,
verses.rendering: The LORD spoke to Moses, instructing him:
translator_notes: The standard divine speech formula vayedabber YHVH el-Mosheh le'mor introduces a major legislative section on the sacrificial calendar. This formula (dibber, 'spoke') signals authoritative instruction rather than casual conversation.

verses.text_hebrew: צַ֚ו אֶת־בְּנֵ֣י יִשְׂרָאֵ֔ל וְאָמַרְתָּ֖ אֲלֵהֶ֑ם אֶת־קׇרְבָּנִ֨י לַחְמִ֜י לְאִשַּׁ֗י רֵ֚יחַ נִֽיחֹחִ֔י תִּשְׁמְר֕וּ לְהַקְרִ֥יב לִ֖י בְּמוֹעֲדֽוֹ׃
verses.text_kjv: Command the children of Israel, and say unto them, My offering, and my bread for my sacrifices made by fire, for a sweet savour unto me, shall ye observe to offer unto me in their due season.
verses.rendering: Direct the Israelites and tell them: You must be careful to present My offering — My food for My fire-offerings, a pleasing aroma to Me — at its appointed time.
translator_notes: The term qorbani ('My offering') derives from qarav ('to draw near'), emphasizing approach to God rather than mere sacrifice. The phrase lachmi ('My food/bread') uses anthropomorphic language — God does not eat, but the metaphor conveys that offerings sustain the covenantal relationship. The term ishai ('My fire-offerings') and re'ach nichochi ('a pleasing aroma to Me') together express divine acceptance. The word bemo'ado ('at its appointed time') introduces the calendrical framework for the entire section.

key_terms.semantic_range: offering, sacrifice, that which is brought near
key_terms.note: From qarav ('to draw near'); the offering is fundamentally about approach to God's presence
Traditionally linked to esh ('fire'), though some scholars derive it from a root meaning 'gift'

key_terms.semantic_range: soothing scent, pleasant fragrance, aroma of satisfaction
key_terms.note: Anthropomorphic language expressing God's favorable acceptance of the offering

verses.text_hebrew: וְאָמַרְתָּ֣ לָהֶ֔ם זֶ֚ה הָֽאִשֶּׁ֔ה אֲשֶׁ֥ר תַּקְרִ֖יבוּ לַיהֹוָ֑ה כְּבָשִׂ֨ים בְּנֵֽי־שָׁנָ֧ה תְמִימִ֛ם שְׁנַ֥יִם לַיּ֖וֹם עֹלָ֥ה תָמִֽיד׃
verses.text_kjv: And thou shalt say unto them, This is the offering made by fire which ye shall offer unto the LORD; two lambs of the first year without spot day by day, for a continual burnt offering.
verses.rendering: Say to them: This is the fire-offering you are to present to the LORD — two unblemished yearling lambs each day as a perpetual ascending offering.
translator_notes: The kevashim benei-shanah ('yearling lambs') must be temimim ('unblemished, complete') — physically whole animals reflecting the wholeness required in worship. The phrase olah tamid ('perpetual ascending offering') establishes the baseline sacrificial rhythm upon which all other offerings are layered. The olah ('ascending offering') was entirely consumed on the altar, symbolizing total dedication.

key_terms.semantic_range: burnt offering, whole offering, that which goes up
key_terms.note: From alah ('to go up, ascend'); the entire animal ascends as smoke to God
Indicates an offering performed without interruption, establishing the baseline rhythm of Israelite worship

verses.text_hebrew: אֶת־הַכֶּ֥בֶשׂ אֶחָ֖ד תַּעֲשֶׂ֣ה בַבֹּ֑קֶר וְאֵת֙ הַכֶּ֣בֶשׂ הַשֵּׁנִ֔י תַּעֲשֶׂ֖ה בֵּ֥ין הָֽעַרְבָּֽיִם׃
verses.text_kjv: The one lamb shalt thou offer in the morning, and the other lamb shalt thou offer at even;
verses.rendering: Prepare one lamb in the morning and the second lamb at twilight,
translator_notes: The Hebrew bein ha'arbayim ('between the two evenings') is a debated time marker — it may refer to the period between afternoon decline and sunset, or between sunset and full darkness. The Pharisaic tradition placed it in the mid-afternoon. This division of the day into morning and twilight sacrifices bookends the daylight hours with worship.

verses.text_hebrew: וַעֲשִׂירִ֧ית הָאֵיפָ֛ה סֹ֖לֶת לְמִנְחָ֑ה בְּלוּלָ֛ה בְּשֶׁ֥מֶן כָּתִ֖ית רְבִיעִ֥ת הַהִֽין׃
verses.text_kjv: And a tenth part of an ephah of flour for a meat offering, mingled with the fourth part of an hin of beaten oil.
verses.rendering: along with a tenth of an ephah of fine flour as a grain offering, mixed with a quarter hin of pressed olive oil.
translator_notes: The solet ('fine flour') denotes the highest quality milled grain. The KJV's 'meat offering' for minchah is misleading — it is a grain-based offering. The shemen katit ('beaten/pressed oil') specifies premium cold-pressed olive oil. A tenth of an ephah is approximately 2.2 liters of flour; a quarter hin is approximately 1.5 liters of oil.

key_terms.semantic_range: gift, tribute, grain offering, meal offering
key_terms.note: KJV's 'meat offering' is anachronistic; minchah in sacrificial contexts refers to flour/grain offerings
The finest grade of milled wheat, sifted to remove coarse particles

verses.text_hebrew: עֹלַ֖ת תָּמִ֑יד הָעֲשֻׂיָה֙ בְּהַ֣ר סִינַ֔י לְרֵ֣יחַ נִיחֹ֔חַ אִשֶּׁ֖ה לַֽיהֹוָֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: It is a continual burnt offering, which was ordained in mount Sinai for a sweet savour, a sacrifice made by fire unto the LORD.
verses.rendering: This is the perpetual ascending offering that was established at Mount Sinai — a pleasing aroma, a fire-offering to the LORD.
translator_notes: The phrase ha'asuyah behar Sinai ('that was made/established at Mount Sinai') anchors this legislation in the Sinai covenant, grounding the sacrificial calendar in the foundational revelation. The tamid offering is not new but a reaffirmation of what was already instituted (cf. Exodus 29:38-42).

verses.text_hebrew: וְנִסְכּוֹ֙ רְבִיעִ֣ת הַהִ֔ין לַכֶּ֖בֶשׂ הָאֶחָ֑ד בַּקֹּ֗דֶשׁ הַסֵּ֛ךְ נֶ֥סֶךְ שֵׁכָ֖ר לַיהֹוָֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: And the drink offering thereof shall be the fourth part of an hin for the one lamb: in the holy place shalt thou cause the strong wine to be poured unto the LORD for a drink offering.
verses.rendering: Its accompanying drink offering is a quarter hin for each lamb. Pour out this fermented drink offering to the LORD in the holy place.
translator_notes: The nesekh ('drink offering, libation') was poured out, not consumed. The term shekhar ('strong drink, fermented beverage') is striking here — it normally appears in prohibitions (Leviticus 10:9). Its use in a sacrificial context suggests a potent fermented grape or grain beverage. The phrase baqqodesh ('in the holy place') specifies the sanctuary location for the pouring.

key_terms.note: From nasakh ('to pour out'); the liquid offering was poured at the altar base

key_terms.semantic_range: strong drink, beer, fermented beverage, intoxicant
key_terms.note: Unusual in a sacrificial prescription; possibly refers to aged wine or barley beer

verses.text_hebrew: וְאֵת֙ הַכֶּ֣בֶשׂ הַשֵּׁנִ֔י תַּעֲשֶׂ֖ה בֵּ֣ין הָֽעַרְבָּ֑יִם כְּמִנְחַ֨ת הַבֹּ֤קֶר וּכְנִסְכּוֹ֙ תַּעֲשֶׂ֔ה אִשֵּׁ֛ה רֵ֥יחַ נִיחֹ֖חַ לַיהֹוָֽה׃ {פ}
verses.text_kjv: And the other lamb shalt thou offer at even: as the meat offering of the morning, and as the drink offering thereof, thou shalt offer it, a sacrifice made by fire, of a sweet savour unto the LORD.
verses.rendering: Prepare the second lamb at twilight with the same grain offering and drink offering as the morning — a fire-offering, a pleasing aroma to the LORD.
translator_notes: The evening offering mirrors the morning one exactly: keminchath habboqer ukhenisku ('like the grain offering of the morning and like its drink offering'). This symmetrical structure frames each day in sacrificial worship, creating a rhythm of perpetual dedication. The parashah petuchah marker {פ} indicates a major section break in the scroll.

verses.text_hebrew: וּבְיוֹם֙ הַשַּׁבָּ֔ת שְׁנֵֽי־כְבָשִׂ֥ים בְּנֵֽי־שָׁנָ֖ה תְּמִימִ֑ם וּשְׁנֵ֣י עֶשְׂרֹנִ֗ים סֹ֧לֶת מִנְחָ֛ה בְּלוּלָ֥ה בַשֶּׁ֖מֶן וְנִסְכּֽוֹ׃
verses.text_kjv: And on the sabbath day two lambs of the first year without spot, and two tenth deals of flour for a meat offering, mingled with oil, and the drink offering thereof:
verses.rendering: On the Sabbath day: two unblemished yearling lambs, with two-tenths of an ephah of fine flour mixed with oil as a grain offering, plus its drink offering.
translator_notes: The Sabbath musaf ('additional offering') doubles the daily tamid — two extra lambs on top of the regular two. The shenei esronim ('two-tenths') of solet doubles the daily grain portion as well. This doubling reflects the Sabbath's elevated sanctity; the day receives twice the worship investment of ordinary days.

verses.text_hebrew: עֹלַ֥ת שַׁבַּ֖ת בְּשַׁבַּתּ֑וֹ עַל־עֹלַ֥ת הַתָּמִ֖יד וְנִסְכָּֽהּ׃ {פ}
verses.text_kjv: This is the burnt offering of every sabbath, beside the continual burnt offering, and his drink offering.
verses.rendering: This is the ascending offering for each Sabbath, in addition to the perpetual ascending offering and its drink offering.
translator_notes: The phrase olat Shabbat beShabbato ('the ascending offering of each Sabbath on its Sabbath') uses a distributive construction — every single Sabbath requires this additional offering. Critically, al-olat hatamid ('in addition to the perpetual ascending offering') establishes the layering principle: festival offerings supplement, never replace, the daily tamid.

verses.text_hebrew: וּבְרָאשֵׁי֙ חׇדְשֵׁיכֶ֔ם תַּקְרִ֥יבוּ עֹלָ֖ה לַיהֹוָ֑ה פָּרִ֨ים בְּנֵֽי־בָקָ֤ר שְׁנַ֙יִם֙ וְאַ֣יִל אֶחָ֔ד כְּבָשִׂ֧ים בְּנֵי־שָׁנָ֛ה שִׁבְעָ֖ה תְּמִימִֽם׃
verses.text_kjv: And in the beginnings of your months ye shall offer a burnt offering unto the LORD; two young bullocks, and one ram, seven lambs of the first year without spot;
verses.rendering: At the start of each of your months, present an ascending offering to the LORD: two young bulls, one ram, and seven unblemished yearling lambs.
translator_notes: The phrase uverashei chodsheikhem ('at the heads of your months') refers to Rosh Chodesh, the new moon celebration marking each lunar month's beginning. The offering escalates dramatically from the daily tamid — parim benei-vaqar shenayim ('two young bulls'), a single ayil ('ram'), and shiv'ah kevashim ('seven lambs'). This substantial increase signals the elevated significance of monthly renewal.

key_terms.rendered_as: start of each of your months
key_terms.semantic_range: heads of your months, new moons, month-beginnings
key_terms.note: The Hebrew calendar is lunar; each month begins with the sighting of the new moon crescent

verses.text_hebrew: וּשְׁלֹשָׁ֣ה עֶשְׂרֹנִ֗ים סֹ֤לֶת מִנְחָה֙ בְּלוּלָ֣ה בַשֶּׁ֔מֶן לַפָּ֖ר הָאֶחָ֑ד וּשְׁנֵ֣י עֶשְׂרֹנִ֗ים סֹ֤לֶת מִנְחָה֙ בְּלוּלָ֣ה בַשֶּׁ֔מֶן לָאַ֖יִל הָֽאֶחָֽד׃
verses.text_kjv: And three tenth deals of flour for a meat offering, mingled with oil, for one bullock; and two tenth deals of flour for a meat offering, mingled with oil, for one ram;
verses.rendering: The grain offering is three-tenths of an ephah of fine flour mixed with oil for each bull, and two-tenths of fine flour mixed with oil for the ram.
translator_notes: The grain offering scales proportionally to the animal's size: sheloshah esronim ('three-tenths') for each par ('bull'), shenei esronim ('two-tenths') for each ayil ('ram'). This proportional system — three for a bull, two for a ram, one for a lamb (v. 13) — maintains a consistent ratio throughout the sacrificial calendar.

verses.text_hebrew: וְעִשָּׂרֹ֣ן עִשָּׂר֗וֹן סֹ֤לֶת מִנְחָה֙ בְּלוּלָ֣ה בַשֶּׁ֔מֶן לַכֶּ֖בֶשׂ הָאֶחָ֑ד עֹלָה֙ רֵ֣יחַ נִיחֹ֔חַ אִשֶּׁ֖ה לַיהֹוָֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: And a several tenth deal of flour mingled with oil for a meat offering unto one lamb; for a burnt offering of a sweet savour, a sacrifice made by fire unto the LORD.
verses.rendering: And one-tenth of fine flour mixed with oil as a grain offering for each lamb — an ascending offering, a pleasing aroma, a fire-offering to the LORD.
translator_notes: The phrase issaron issaron ('a tenth, a tenth') uses repetition distributively — one-tenth of an ephah for each individual lamb. This completes the proportional grain offering scale: 3:2:1 for bull:ram:lamb. The verse concludes with the full formula: olah re'ach nichoach issheh laYHVH, affirming that each offering is acceptable to God.

verses.text_hebrew: וְנִסְכֵּיהֶ֗ם חֲצִ֣י הַהִין֩ יִהְיֶ֨ה לַפָּ֜ר וּשְׁלִישִׁ֧ת הַהִ֣ין לָאַ֗יִל וּרְבִיעִ֥ת הַהִ֛ין לַכֶּ֖בֶשׂ יָ֑יִן זֹ֣את עֹלַ֥ת חֹ֙דֶשׁ֙ בְּחׇדְשׁ֔וֹ לְחׇדְשֵׁ֖י הַשָּׁנָֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: And their drink offerings shall be half an hin of wine unto a bullock, and the third part of an hin unto a ram, and a fourth part of an hin unto a lamb: this is the burnt offering of every month throughout the months of the year.
verses.rendering: Their drink offerings are: half a hin of wine for each bull, a third of a hin for the ram, and a quarter hin for each lamb. This is the monthly ascending offering for every month throughout the year.
translator_notes: The drink offerings follow their own proportional scale: chatzi hahin ('half a hin', ~3 liters) for a bull, shelishit hahin ('a third', ~2 liters) for a ram, revi'it hahin ('a quarter', ~1.5 liters) for a lamb. The yayin ('wine') is specified here rather than the shekhar of verse 7. The phrase olat chodesh bechodsho lechodshei hashanah ('monthly ascending offering for each month of the year') mandates twelve such celebrations annually.

verses.text_hebrew: וּשְׂעִ֨יר עִזִּ֥ים אֶחָ֛ד לְחַטָּ֖את לַיהֹוָ֑ה עַל־עֹלַ֧ת הַתָּמִ֛יד יֵעָשֶׂ֖ה וְנִסְכּֽוֹ׃ {ס}
verses.text_kjv: And one kid of the goats for a sin offering unto the LORD shall be offered, beside the continual burnt offering, and his drink offering.
verses.rendering: Also one male goat as a purification offering to the LORD — this is in addition to the perpetual ascending offering with its drink offering.
translator_notes: The se'ir izzim ('male goat') serves as a chatat ('purification/sin offering'). The chatat addresses communal impurity that accumulates over the month. The layering principle is reiterated: al-olat hatamid ('in addition to the perpetual ascending offering') — the new moon offerings do not substitute for but supplement the daily tamid.

key_terms.semantic_range: sin offering, purification offering, decontamination offering
key_terms.note: From chata ('to miss the mark, sin'); the offering purges ritual contamination and moral guilt from the sanctuary

verses.text_hebrew: וּבַחֹ֣דֶשׁ הָרִאשׁ֗וֹן בְּאַרְבָּעָ֥ה עָשָׂ֛ר י֖וֹם לַחֹ֑דֶשׁ פֶּ֖סַח לַיהֹוָֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: And in the fourteenth day of the first month is the passover of the LORD.
verses.rendering: On the fourteenth day of the first month, the Passover belonging to the LORD is observed.
translator_notes: The Pesach ('Passover') falls on the fourteenth of the first month (Nisan/Aviv). The term pesach likely derives from pasach ('to pass over, skip over'), recalling how God passed over Israelite homes during the tenth plague. This verse states the date without offering instructions because the Passover lamb follows its own legislation (Exodus 12); what follows concerns the week-long festival of Unleavened Bread.

key_terms.note: The foundational redemptive festival commemorating the exodus from Egypt

verses.text_hebrew: וּבַחֲמִשָּׁ֨ה עָשָׂ֥ר י֛וֹם לַחֹ֥דֶשׁ הַזֶּ֖ה חָ֑ג שִׁבְעַ֣ת יָמִ֔ים מַצּ֖וֹת יֵאָכֵֽל׃
verses.text_kjv: And in the fifteenth day of this month is the feast: seven days shall unleavened bread be eaten.
verses.rendering: On the fifteenth day of this month is the festival. For seven days unleavened bread is to be eaten.
translator_notes: The chag ('festival, pilgrimage feast') begins the day after Passover. The matzot ('unleavened bread') must be eaten for shiv'at yamim ('seven days'). Leaven symbolizes the old life in Egypt; its removal represents the haste of departure and the purging of corruption. The passive construction ye'akhel ('shall be eaten') makes this a universal obligation.

key_terms.note: Bread made without se'or (leaven/sourdough starter), commemorating the hasty exodus

verses.text_hebrew: בַּיּ֥וֹם הָרִאשׁ֖וֹן מִקְרָא־קֹ֑דֶשׁ כׇּל־מְלֶ֥אכֶת עֲבֹדָ֖ה לֹ֥א תַעֲשֽׂוּ׃
verses.text_kjv: In the first day shall be an holy convocation; ye shall do no manner of servile work therein:
verses.rendering: On the first day there is to be a sacred assembly. You must not perform any occupational labor.
translator_notes: The miqra-qodesh ('sacred assembly, holy convocation') is a called gathering for worship. The prohibition covers kol-melekhet avodah ('any laborious work, occupational work') — this phrase distinguishes festival rest from full Sabbath rest, which prohibits kol-melakhah ('all work'). Food preparation is generally permitted on festival days but not on the Sabbath.

key_terms.semantic_range: holy convocation, sacred gathering, called assembly
key_terms.note: From qara ('to call'); a summoned gathering set apart for worship

verses.text_hebrew: וְהִקְרַבְתֶּ֨ם אִשֶּׁ֤ה עֹלָה֙ לַֽיהֹוָ֔ה פָּרִ֧ים בְּנֵי־בָקָ֛ר שְׁנַ֖יִם וְאַ֣יִל אֶחָ֑ד וְשִׁבְעָ֤ה כְבָשִׂים֙ בְּנֵ֣י שָׁנָ֔ה תְּמִימִ֖ם יִהְי֥וּ לָכֶֽם׃
verses.text_kjv: But ye shall offer a sacrifice made by fire for a burnt offering unto the LORD; two young bullocks, and one ram, and seven lambs of the first year: they shall be unto you without blemish:
verses.rendering: Present a fire-offering as an ascending offering to the LORD: two young bulls, one ram, and seven yearling lambs — they must be unblemished for your offering.
translator_notes: The Passover/Matzot offerings match the new moon offerings exactly (v. 11): parim shenayim ('two bulls'), ayil echad ('one ram'), shiv'ah khevashim benei shanah ('seven yearling lambs'). The phrase temimim yihyu lakhem ('they shall be unblemished for you') places the responsibility for quality inspection on the offerers.

verses.text_hebrew: וּמִ֨נְחָתָ֔ם סֹ֖לֶת בְּלוּלָ֣ה בַשָּׁ֑מֶן שְׁלֹשָׁ֨ה עֶשְׂרֹנִ֜ים לַפָּ֗ר וּשְׁנֵ֧י עֶשְׂרֹנִ֛ים לָאַ֖יִל תַּעֲשֽׂוּ׃
verses.text_kjv: And their meat offering shall be of flour mingled with oil: three tenth deals shall ye offer for a bullock, and two tenth deals for a ram;
verses.rendering: Their grain offering is fine flour mixed with oil: prepare three-tenths of an ephah for each bull and two-tenths for the ram.
translator_notes: The minchatam ('their grain offering') follows the standard proportional formula established in verses 12-13: sheloshah esronim laPar ('three-tenths for each bull') and shenei esronim la'ayil ('two-tenths for the ram'). The consistency of these ratios across different festivals underscores the systematic nature of Israelite sacrificial legislation.

verses.text_hebrew: עִשָּׂר֤וֹן עִשָּׂרוֹן֙ תַּעֲשֶׂ֔ה לַכֶּ֖בֶשׂ הָאֶחָ֑ד לְשִׁבְעַ֖ת הַכְּבָשִֽׂים׃
verses.text_kjv: A several tenth deal shalt thou offer for every lamb, throughout the seven lambs:
verses.rendering: Prepare one-tenth for each individual lamb across all seven lambs.
translator_notes: The distributive issaron issaron ('a tenth, a tenth') emphasizes that each of the shiv'at hakkevashim ('seven lambs') receives its own separate grain portion. The repetition ensures no confusion: seven individual tenth-measures, not one communal portion divided among them.

verses.text_hebrew: וּשְׂעִ֥יר חַטָּ֖את אֶחָ֑ד לְכַפֵּ֖ר עֲלֵיכֶֽם׃
verses.text_kjv: And one goat for a sin offering, to make an atonement for you.
verses.rendering: Also one male goat as a purification offering, to make atonement on your behalf.
translator_notes: The se'ir chatat echad ('one male goat as purification offering') serves lekhapper aleikhem ('to make atonement/covering for you'). The root k-p-r carries the sense of 'covering over' or 'wiping clean' — the offering purges accumulated impurity so the community can stand in God's presence during the festival.

key_terms.semantic_range: atone, cover, purge, ransom, make expiation
key_terms.note: The central verb of Israel's purification system; debate persists whether the primary sense is 'to cover,' 'to wipe clean,' or 'to ransom'

verses.text_hebrew: מִלְּבַד֙ עֹלַ֣ת הַבֹּ֔קֶר אֲשֶׁ֖ר לְעֹלַ֣ת הַתָּמִ֑יד תַּעֲשׂ֖וּ אֶת־אֵֽלֶּה׃
verses.text_kjv: Ye shall offer these beside the burnt offering in the morning, which is for a continual burnt offering.
verses.rendering: You are to prepare these in addition to the morning ascending offering that constitutes the perpetual offering.
translator_notes: The phrase millevad olat habboqer ('apart from the morning ascending offering') reiterates the layering principle: the festival offerings are supplementary. The relative clause asher le'olat hatamid ('which belongs to the perpetual offering') identifies the morning lamb as part of the ongoing tamid system. Festival worship intensifies but never interrupts the daily rhythm.

verses.text_hebrew: כָּאֵ֜לֶּה תַּעֲשׂ֤וּ לַיּוֹם֙ שִׁבְעַ֣ת יָמִ֔ים לֶ֛חֶם אִשֵּׁ֥ה רֵֽיחַ־נִיחֹ֖חַ לַיהֹוָ֑ה עַל־עוֹלַ֧ת הַתָּמִ֛יד יֵעָשֶׂ֖ה וְנִסְכּֽוֹ׃
verses.text_kjv: After this manner ye shall offer daily, throughout the seven days, the meat of the sacrifice made by fire, of a sweet savour unto the LORD: it shall be offered beside the continual burnt offering, and his drink offering.
verses.rendering: In this same manner you are to prepare the offering each day for seven days — food of the fire-offering, a pleasing aroma to the LORD. It is to be prepared in addition to the perpetual ascending offering and its drink offering.
translator_notes: The phrase ka'elleh ta'asu layyom shiv'at yamim ('according to these you shall prepare daily for seven days') mandates that the full Matzot offering sequence repeats for the entire festival week. The term lechem ('food, bread') here refers to the offering as God's 'food' — the same anthropomorphic metaphor from verse 2. The total over seven days is substantial: 14 bulls, 7 rams, 49 lambs, plus 7 goats.

verses.text_hebrew: וּבַיּוֹם֙ הַשְּׁבִיעִ֔י מִקְרָא־קֹ֖דֶשׁ יִהְיֶ֣ה לָכֶ֑ם כׇּל־מְלֶ֥אכֶת עֲבֹדָ֖ה לֹ֥א תַעֲשֽׂוּ׃ {ס}
verses.text_kjv: And on the seventh day ye shall have an holy convocation; ye shall do no servile work.
verses.rendering: On the seventh day you are to hold a sacred assembly. You must not perform any occupational labor.
translator_notes: The festival week concludes as it began (v. 18) with a miqra-qodesh ('sacred assembly') and the prohibition of melekhet avodah ('occupational labor'). This bookending structure — sacred assembly on days one and seven — frames the entire festival as a unified period of consecration. The setumah marker {ס} signals a minor section break.

verses.text_hebrew: וּבְי֣וֹם הַבִּכּוּרִ֗ים בְּהַקְרִ֨יבְכֶ֜ם מִנְחָ֤ה חֲדָשָׁה֙ לַֽיהֹוָ֔ה בְּשָׁבֻעֹ֖תֵיכֶ֑ם מִֽקְרָא־קֹ֙דֶשׁ֙ יִהְיֶ֣ה לָכֶ֔ם כׇּל־מְלֶ֥אכֶת עֲבֹדָ֖ה לֹ֥א תַעֲשֽׂוּ׃
verses.text_kjv: Also in the day of the firstfruits, when ye bring a new meat offering unto the LORD, after your weeks be out, ye shall have an holy convocation; ye shall do no servile work:
verses.rendering: On the Day of Firstfruits, when you present a new grain offering to the LORD at your Festival of Weeks, hold a sacred assembly. You must not perform any occupational labor.
translator_notes: The yom haBikkurim ('Day of Firstfruits') is also called Shavuot ('Weeks') — beshavu'oteikhem ('at your weeks') refers to the seven-week count from Passover (Leviticus 23:15-16). The minchah chadashah ('new grain offering') features the first wheat of the harvest. This verse provides three names for one festival: Firstfruits, Weeks, and (in later tradition) Pentecost (from the Greek for 'fiftieth').

key_terms.note: From bakhar or bekor ('firstborn'); the initial yield of the harvest dedicated to God

key_terms.semantic_range: weeks, periods of seven, Feast of Weeks
key_terms.note: Named for the seven weeks counted from the waving of the barley sheaf at Passover

verses.text_hebrew: וְהִקְרַבְתֶּ֨ם עוֹלָ֜ה לְרֵ֤יחַ נִיחֹ֙חַ֙ לַֽיהֹוָ֔ה פָּרִ֧ים בְּנֵי־בָקָ֛ר שְׁנַ֖יִם אַ֣יִל אֶחָ֑ד שִׁבְעָ֥ה כְבָשִׂ֖ים בְּנֵ֥י שָׁנָֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: But ye shall offer the burnt offering for a sweet savour unto the LORD; two young bullocks, one ram, seven lambs of the first year;
verses.rendering: Present an ascending offering as a pleasing aroma to the LORD: two young bulls, one ram, and seven yearling lambs.
translator_notes: The Shavuot offering matches the Passover/Matzot and new moon offerings in quantity: parim shenayim ('two bulls'), ayil echad ('one ram'), shiv'ah kevashim ('seven yearling lambs'). This uniformity among the spring festivals (excluding Sukkot's distinctive escalation in chapter 29) creates a consistent tier of worship for the pilgrimage festivals.

verses.text_hebrew: וּמִ֨נְחָתָ֔ם סֹ֖לֶת בְּלוּלָ֣ה בַשָּׁ֑מֶן שְׁלֹשָׁ֨ה עֶשְׂרֹנִ֜ים לַפָּ֣ר הָֽאֶחָ֔ד שְׁנֵי֙ עֶשְׂרֹנִ֔ים לָאַ֖יִל הָאֶחָֽד׃
verses.text_kjv: And their meat offering of flour mingled with oil, three tenth deals unto one bullock, two tenth deals unto one ram,
verses.rendering: Their grain offering of fine flour mixed with oil: three-tenths of an ephah for each bull and two-tenths for the ram.
translator_notes: The minchatam solet belulah bashshamen ('their grain offering of fine flour mixed with oil') repeats the standard formula. The proportions remain constant: sheloshah esronim laPar ha'echad ('three-tenths for each bull') and shenei esronim la'ayil ha'echad ('two-tenths for the one ram'). The definite articles (ha'echad) individualize each animal's portion.

verses.text_hebrew: עִשָּׂרוֹן֙ עִשָּׂר֔וֹן לַכֶּ֖בֶשׂ הָאֶחָ֑ד לְשִׁבְעַ֖ת הַכְּבָשִֽׂים׃
verses.text_kjv: A several tenth deal unto one lamb, throughout the seven lambs;
verses.rendering: One-tenth for each individual lamb across the seven lambs.
translator_notes: The distributive issaron issaron ('a tenth, a tenth') is repeated from verse 21 — lakkevesh ha'echad ('for each individual lamb') across leshiv'at hakkevashim ('the seven lambs'). Seven separate grain portions, one per lamb, totaling seven-tenths of an ephah for the lamb offerings alone.

verses.text_hebrew: שְׂעִ֥יר עִזִּ֖ים אֶחָ֑ד לְכַפֵּ֖ר עֲלֵיכֶֽם׃
verses.text_kjv: And one kid of the goats, to make an atonement for you.
verses.rendering: Include one male goat to make atonement on your behalf.
translator_notes: The se'ir izzim echad ('one male goat') for the Shavuot purification offering mirrors verse 22's Passover provision. The verb lekhapper ('to make atonement, to purge') again employs the k-p-r root. Every major festival requires this purification component — communal gathering before God necessitates communal cleansing.

verses.text_hebrew: מִלְּבַ֞ד עֹלַ֧ת הַתָּמִ֛יד וּמִנְחָת֖וֹ תַּעֲשׂ֑וּ תְּמִימִ֥ם יִהְיוּ־לָכֶ֖ם וְנִסְכֵּיהֶֽם׃ {פ}
verses.text_kjv: Ye shall offer them beside the continual burnt offering, and his meat offering, (they shall be unto you without blemish) and their drink offerings.
verses.rendering: Prepare these in addition to the perpetual ascending offering and its grain offering. The animals must be unblemished, along with their drink offerings.
translator_notes: The closing verse reiterates the supplementary nature of festival offerings: millevad olat hatamid uminchat ('apart from the perpetual ascending offering and its grain offering'). The parenthetical temimim yihyu-lakhem ('they shall be unblemished for you') places the quality requirement last as a final emphatic reminder. The parashah petuchah marker {פ} closes the spring festival cycle, with fall festivals continuing in chapter 29.
